full_text: Episode 71 of Cooking Whatever the Top Common Is, and today you chose one of India's most popular street foods, samosa, which has some pretty deep historical roots. Its ancestor is sambusak, a deep-fried pastry filled with minced meat, nuts, and spices. Sounds pretty familiar. Back in the 1200s, they were popular, and they spread from Persia to the Middle East and Central Asia, and to Turkic rulers who established the Delhi Sultanate, an empire that ruled northern India. Their courts spoke Persian, dressed Persian, and ate Persian dishes. Among those dishes was sambusak. Outside the palace walls, Indian cooks were watching. They borrowed the dish but adapted it with local ingredients. Cumin, coriander, chilies. They made the pastry crispier. And very importantly, in the 1500s, a new ingredient arrives from across the ocean, the potato. Since meat was expensive, the filling shifts to spiced potato pretty quickly, and is what allows it to be accessible to the masses. Up until this point, it was royal food. But by the 1800s, it had fully transitioned into street food culture. Portable, cheap, deep-fried, and stands the test of time, the samosa is one of India's most iconic snacks. I'm dipping mine into some mint chutney, and it is absolutely delicious. Kinda hot though, I couldn't wait for him to cool down.
